---
title: "New publication: \"What are you in for?\""
date: 2026-04-10
categories:
  - publication
  - news
description: "Announcing publication of a new article examining why prison researchers have largely avoided discussing what people in prison think about their crimes"
keywords:
  - prison sociology
  - moral communication
  - punishment
  - legitimacy
image: "/_assets/marek-mucha-MFiekVrRagM-unsplash.jpg"
toc: false
draft: false
copyright:
  holder: © Ben Jarman
  year: 2026
---

![]({{< meta image >}})

::: {.callout-note}
Photo by <a href="https://unsplash.com/@marcc">Marek Mucha</a> on <a href="https://unsplash.com/photos/person-wearing-silver-link-bracelet-watch-MFiekVrRagM">Unsplash</a>
:::

I'm pleased to announce the publication of [a new article](/publications/jarmanWhatAreYou2026.qmd) in the journal *Incarceration*. It's titled *"What are you in for?" The neglected role of the offence in prison sociology*, and is co-authored by me, Alice Ievins (University of Liverpool) and Ben Crewe (University of Cambridge).

The article addresses a striking omission in prison research: that scholars have typically avoided asking people in prison what they think about their offences, or examining how these shape their experience of imprisonment. This, we argue, has left a significant blind spot in our understanding of what prisons actually do and how they are experienced.

Drawing on three detailed case studies, we show how the offence—what someone did, or is deemed to have done—plays an active role in daily prison life. For some people in prison, the primary struggle is not social survival in a difficult environment, but rather an internal, psychological battle to come to terms with shame, bewilderment, or a more complex mixture of feelings about why they are there. In our view, how people relate to the offence can influence:

- How people in prison relate to staff and each other
- Whether and how they engage with rehabilitation programmes
- The intensity of their emotional and moral struggles during imprisonment
- How they experience punishment itself

We develop a line of thinking that has featured differently in each author's work to date: that prisons operate as institutions of moral communication, conveying messages about wrongdoing and responsibility. Yet this communication is often inconsistent, oblique, and distorted. Some people in prison never receive the censure that sentencers intended; others are overwhelmed by shame that makes reflection upon it difficult or impossible; still others find that prison impedes the very moral work—such as making amends to victims—that punishment is claimed to promote.

This leads us to identify tensions in how contemporary prisons operate. They initiate moral processes they cannot complete, leaving people at various stages of grappling with responsibility and remorse, and the incomplete work of censure.

## Implications for prison research

We propose a framework for recentring the offence in prison sociology, examining its influence in three dimensions:

1. **What prisons are like**: Understanding prisoners' subjective experiences requires attention to their moral struggles with the offence, not just their social adaptations
2. **What prisons do**: Recognising prisons as sites of moral communication, whether intended or not
3. **What prisons are for**: Engaging with normative debates about punishment's purposes and whether prisons can achieve them

Our aim is for this work to contribute to ongoing debates about penal legitimacy, the justification of punishment, and the ethics of imprisonment. By bringing empirical complexity to these discussions, we hope to challenge both punitive rhetoric ("bad places for bad people") and naïve rehabilitation fantasies ("using prisons to reduce reoffending"), suggesting that both of these perspectives overlook what actually happens in prisons.

## Availability

The article is Open Access and is available at the journal's website [here](https://dx.doi.org/10.1177/26326663261442087). You can also read the full article text on this website by clicking [here](/publications/jarmanWhatAreYou2026.qmd).
